## [03:02 - 06:06]

But actually at the same time, I was all the time talking about something else. I was purposefully choosing that story in order to trick your brain into sort of opening up and for you to make that cognitive leap from one side to the other. And one of the things I know about surprise, which is one of the reasons I love it so much is surprise when the brain is surprised when a human being is surprised, brain plasticity actually increases, which is to say, if you surprise someone, you sort of prime their brain for the greatest amount of learning at that moment. Is there opportunity to genuinely change structures in the brain and cause them to think differently? And I think sometimes it is almost impossible in this world sometimes to get people to genuinely change an opinion on something that they really believe in. And I think one of the only ways you can sort of get people to change their minds is by creating that opportunity for surprise. In other words, you get someone to let their guard down by telling them a story that is entertaining and perhaps funny or vulnerable or heartfelt or maybe even make them cry. And it's in that moment where their guard is down because they think you're telling a story to entertain them and to make them laugh and to make them feel good. And it's in that singular moment that you let them know, I was actually speaking about something else. And because it's surprising and because their guard is down, I think that's sort of the only time you can get people to truly change their mind about something powerful and meaningful in their lives. And I do that with students all the time now admittedly they're 10. So their opinions are not quite as hardened as let's say a 39 year old man who has a, it's a much harder lift, I would say. But thankfully in most of the work we do and most of the business that people do, we're not looking to sort of flip a liberal to a conservative or flip a Catholic to a Jew. We're really looking to get people to believe that our product is superior to someone else's product or our service is better than someone else's service or our idea in terms of the direction the company should be moving or our idea in terms of how leadership should be structured or our core values that we are proposing for our company. Those are a little more malleable, I'd say, they're easier to move people. It's easier to get to move people from Kellogg's Raisin brand to Post Raisin brand than it is to get someone to believe that the Celtics are better than the Lakers. That is a much harder lift. I don't think it's impossible. I do think that those things are possible, but I think it's a lot more difficult. But I do believe that when you speak with adjacency and you get people to let their guards down and surprise them, then brain plasticity increases considerably. And it's in that moment of surprise that people actually can take an objective view on something and perhaps change their mind in a deep and meaningful way, which is why I do it all the time. It's why no matter who I'm speaking to, I'm constantly kind of doing that, thinking how can I speak about the thing that I want to speak

## [06:06 - 09:07]

about without speaking about the thing I want to speak about. It's interesting because the first time I began doing this was when I was telling stories for entertainment purposes only. I quickly realized, probably at a young age, but especially when I started telling stories at the Moth 14 years ago, that the story format, the type of story that one more Moth story slams than any other, was what my wife came to call the laugh, laugh, laugh, cry model of storytelling, which is to say, I construct a story that is deliberately, purposefully, and aggressively amusing in the first three quarters of the story. Maybe even the first eight ninths of the story is sort of taking people down a primrose path, taking people down a path that they think is entertaining and enjoyable and lovely and delightful. And then at the end of that story or closing in on the end, I indicate to them in a really meaningful way that I was actually not talking about that at all. We were actually heading in another direction entirely. And so that is predicated on surprise, which is to say we need to oftentimes lay down some bricks or lay down some clues or lay down some touchstones earlier in the story so that they pay off later. One of the best examples of this, and it is sort of speaking through adjacency, is a story I tell called The Promise, and I hate titling stories, forgive me, but they have to be titled sometimes so I can refer to them. But it is about my high school girlfriend. And it's a story that pretends to be a story about trying to get my first kiss with my high school girlfriend, which is amusing because I fail miserably a multitude of times. And then finally, in sort of a sweaty, smelly staircase in a siesta hotel in Pasadena, California, right before the Rose Bowl, we're marching in the Rose Bowl. We live in Massachusetts, but we've flown to California for the Rose Bowl. And I've been trying to kiss her for like three weeks and I'm just miserable at all of this. And so she's been waiting for me to kiss her. I mean, three weeks into a relationship, you haven't kissed a girl, you've got a problem at that point, that is a disaster. And so finally, she gets tired of waiting for me to kiss her. And so she pulls me in, in this stairwell in this hotel that we're staying in, and she kisses me. And then she says to me, she says, can we get on with things now? And it's sort of a beautiful moment in a terrible location. And it is also in the midst of a failure on my part. But that's not what the story is about, right? So the story is about how when we begin dating, just before I try to start getting that first kiss, we make a promise to each other. We're friends at the time. We're very, very good friends. And we just started to start dating. And we make a promise on these steps outside the band room that no matter what happens, we'll always stay friends, even if this relationship doesn't work out. And so it's a laugh, laugh, laugh, laugh, laugh, laugh, cry story, meaning you think you're in the midst of one story and then suddenly it becomes another story at the end. And that is speaking through adjacency. Now that's, I would say storytelling through adjacency, which is you think you're in one story, but now I'm flipping you to a new story. But that's essentially what speaking through adjacency is. It's instead of sort of flipping the contours of a story from one path to another, we are simply taking whatever the message we want to send to someone. And in taking that message, we're finding a story that matches that message that is sort of as far apart from the message as we as humanly possible. And when we can separate those two, the greater the separation that we can create between the message and the content of the message and the actual story, the more surprise we create. So when that story is so separate from everything else, that's when things work out the best oftentimes. telling the story of the career path, because that would be kind of boring to just say, well, I joined the company here and I moved from this team to that team to this team to that team. Every story should end with surprise.

## [21:22 - 24:23]

Because if your story is about change, change is always surprising to us. Change sneaks up on us. There's never like, I think I'm going to try to change and then you change. Even if you're planning on changing, when it actually happens, you're like, damn, I actually feel differently now. It's kind of shocking. I lost like 40 pounds last year, basically just by riding my bike harder than I was before. Truly, that's all I did was I stopped riding my bike at an easy pace. I just decided to kill myself and I managed to lose 40 pounds doing so. Even though my plan was to lose weight and even though I saw the weight coming off, I was still surprised when I looked in the mirror one day. I was like, damn, I really lost a lot of weight. Even though I cognitively understood it because I was standing on a scale every day. And even though I was catching myself in the mirror from time to time, there was a moment when I went, wow, I actually lost like 40 pounds. I had to change my entire wardrobe. I literally had to buy a brand new wardrobe to accommodate my new body. And yet I was still surprised when I saw it because I think that's the nature of surprise, the nature of change. Even when it happens, it is surprising to some degree to us. I'm still surprised my wife married me. To this day, I have moments where I go, I cannot believe I married that woman. Like how the hell did that ever happen? How did I manage to get that girl to agree to be with this boy for as long as she has been? And that's a genuine surprise. So every story should have surprise at the end. It's the most delightful thing we have to offer an audience. And it's what we want at the end of every story. So yeah, I think that beautiful surprise, that story. Now you asked me another really interesting and challenging question is, how the hell do I say that story in five or six minutes, right? That's a complicated story. So the tricks that I will give you in terms of telling a story that is shorter than what you need it to be or what you want it to be essentially, there's basically two things you can do to shorten a story. One is you can eliminate scenes. That's a little harder, admittedly, but there are times when you can eliminate scenes. So you're just, you're just saying to yourself, I don't need to have this scene in the story. I want it. I love it. It's great. But I don't need it. Right. The story still holds together and makes sense without the scene. So you can, you can look for moments where you don't need to actually say the thing as much as you might want to say it. The other thing you can do, which is what I try to do first is I try to start every scene as far into the scene as possible. Right. And so that means removing process language oftentimes. So that means instead of saying, I drive from the airport to my grandfather's house, you say, I step off the plane. An hour later, I'm knocking on a door. It's my grandfather's house and you eliminate anything that happens in between. And, and actually, maybe you don't even need to be knocking on the door. Maybe it can be an hour later, I'm standing in a kitchen that I grew up in. I can't believe I'm here right now. You're pushed in even further. Now you've gone beyond the door and the introduction. Now you're in the kitchen.

## [24:23 - 27:26]

And you see movies do this all the time. They start as far into a scene as humanly possible, because filmmakers understand that the further you get into a scene, the more interesting that scene is. And the more likely there's going to be a heightened level of suspense, action, excitement, surprise, humor, all of these things. So what I will often do is get as far into a scene as possible. And that shortens every scene. It actually makes most stories better. I don't think that removing entire scenes from stories always makes them better. I think it's sort of as a unfortunate thing that we have to do that sometimes really makes a story not as good, but is necessary to fit some arbitrary time limit imposed upon us by some terrible human being. But I do think that when we aggressively start stories with scenes, we aggressively start scenes further in and we eliminate all process language. I think that makes stories better. So there's nothing wrong with doing that, I think. There's also a habit that I have and other people where I tend to provide three examples for everything. So instead of providing three, I provide one. So I often want three. The magic of three is a powerful thing. Three creates a lot of humor, too, because you can create two things that are expected and then an unexpected one. I call it one of these things is not like the other. You'll hear comedians do it all the time. They just say two things and then they say a thing that's completely different than the other two and it's like from left field that it makes you laugh. But oftentimes those three examples can be whittled down to the one best of the examples. I'd also think that kind of hurts a story a little bit, but it is a way to make a story shorter, unfortunately. Yeah. Thank you. Yeah. In a story like yours, maybe one more thing I might suggest to think about, it's Kevin, right? Kevin? Yeah. So one thing you might want to think about, Kevin, is the restructuring chronologically of a story can make it shorter sometimes. So instead of telling that story chronologically, you could start that story with, I'm knocking on a door. It's a door I haven't seen in a very long time, right? And it opens and someone's behind it and now you step into the house and you're shocked you're in the house and it's a kitchen you're familiar with. And so you move, you start the story that way and then at some point you're going to drop some backstory into explain sort of what I'm doing here. When we drop backstory into a story, that backstory ends up being a lot shorter than if we had actually told it in chronological order. You know, because you just can't backstory a lot and you feel the pain of doing it. So oftentimes creating a B-A-B-C story, which means start in the middle, and then at some point you're going to bring that beginning part into the story, but you're going to bring in this memory or is a reflection or you're telling someone backstory, any way to do that. That often makes a story shorter. It's a good way to tell a story, frankly, especially if the beginning of your story is not terribly interesting. You know, I would be inclined to start your story.

right his father Boris's son's Boris's son his father Boris wants him to go to the Bruins game and every inch of his body every inkling of his body should want to go and yet he doesn't but it's about
